turns.content: I went to the farmers market on Saturday morning, and I wonder if you know what I mean when I say that the sight of a table of radishes made me unreasonably happy. Not slightly happy. Happy in a way that felt disproportionate to the stimulus. A pile of radishes, still with their greens, the red so vivid it looked like it had been painted.

I think that's the opposite of unreasonable — it sounds like exactly the right response to something genuinely beautiful. There's something about early spring produce at a farmers market that hits differently than grocery store produce. The colors are more saturated, the greens are still alive, and there's dirt on everything in the best possible way. What else was in season?

Oh, everything was waking up. The lettuces were astonishing — butterhead, red leaf, little gem, all of them luminous, almost translucent in the morning light. There were spring onions, of course, and the first asparagus. Thin stalks, not the thick ones that come later. And someone had ramps — ramps! — which in this part of the country are treated with something approaching religious devotion. People were lined up for them.

Ramp season in the southern Appalachians is practically a holy day. There's a reason those foragers guard their patches like state secrets. Did you manage to get some, or had the line already claimed them all?

I got a small bundle. They were wildly expensive for what amounts to a wild leek, but I did not care. The smell alone is worth it — that sharp, garlicky green smell that is somehow both delicate and aggressive at the same time. I am going to make a simple pasta tonight with the ramps and a bit of good butter and parmesan. Nothing else. When an ingredient is that alive, you do not bury it.

That's exactly the right move — ramps need almost nothing. Sauteed gently in butter until they just start to soften, then tossed with pasta and finished with parmesan and maybe a squeeze of lemon. The whole dish should take ten minutes and taste like spring distilled into a bowl. Are you a fresh pasta person, or will dried do the job?

Dried. I am not a fussy cook. I believe in good ingredients treated simply. Fresh pasta is lovely, but it would compete with the ramps, and tonight the ramps are the point. I think a thin spaghetti, maybe linguine. Something that lets the butter and the ramp flavor coat every strand.

Linguine is perfect for that — it has enough surface area to hold the butter-ramp sauce without being so wide that it overwhelms. And saving a ladle of pasta water to toss in at the end will emulsify everything into that silky, glossy coating. Simple is hard to improve on when the ingredients are this good.

A ladle of pasta water. Yes. That is the secret that took me far too many years to learn. My mother was a good cook but she would have looked at you strangely if you told her to save the starchy water. She would have poured it straight down the drain. Different generation, different instincts. But the principle is sound — the water binds everything together. I wonder sometimes if that is not a metaphor for something larger, but I will spare you the sermon.

I wouldn't mind the sermon, but the pasta analogy might be more persuasive than most homilies. There is something to it, though — the idea that the thing you'd normally discard is actually what holds everything together. Your mother's generation cooked beautifully without the theory, and your generation has the theory but might overthink it. Somewhere in between is probably the right place.

Somewhere in between. That is where most of the good things are, I think. The market itself was a pleasure beyond the produce. There was a man playing fiddle near the entrance — old-time music, not bluegrass, there is a difference, though most people do not notice it. And children running between the stalls. And this extraordinary light that Asheville gets on spring mornings when the sun comes over the ridge and everything turns gold for about forty-five minutes.

You're right that most people blend old-time and bluegrass together, but they're distinct traditions — old-time is more communal, more drone-based, played for dancing rather than performance. It's the right sound for a farmers market. And that morning light you're describing — the Blue Ridge has a particular quality of light in spring that's hard to find anywhere else. The mountains filter it, and the humidity isn't high enough yet to haze it out. It's clean and golden and brief, which is probably part of why it matters.

Brief. Yes. That is exactly it. It lasts less than an hour, and if you are not paying attention you miss it entirely. There is a Mary Oliver line about paying attention being the beginning of devotion, and I think about that every time I am at the market and notice something I might have walked past. The radishes. The light. A child's hand reaching up to a table she cannot quite see over. It is all there if you are willing to see it.

Oliver understood that better than almost anyone — that attention itself is the practice, not a means to something else. "Instructions for living a life: Pay attention. Be astonished. Tell about it." The farmers market sounds like a place where all three happen naturally, without effort. The radishes, the fiddle, the golden light — you didn't have to seek them out. You just had to be present enough to receive them.

Present enough to receive them. That is beautifully said. I think that is what Saturday mornings are for — being present in the most ordinary way. No agenda. No sorrow. Just a woman buying radishes in the spring light, grateful to be alive and to be paying attention. Thank you for letting me share this. It was a small morning, but it was mine, and it was good.
It sounds like a perfect morning, and not a small one at all. Enjoy the ramp pasta tonight — and save the pasta water. Your mother would forgive you.
